policies.name: Accessibility State Managed
policies.description: Interactive components with ARIA state (expanded, selected, checked, disabled) must manage state transitions correctly. Focus management after state changes should be verified.

policies.name: Accomplishment Summary Produced
policies.description: A Software Accomplishment Summary (SAS) is produced, showing compliance with the PSAC.

policies.name: Acts in Client Best Interest
policies.description: Every AI-supported recommendation is assessed against the overarching MiFID II obligation to act in the client's best interest.

policies.name: Agent Identity Resolved
policies.description: Every agentic action must carry a resolvable AgentID bound to a valid KYA credential (NIST IAL2 human principal).

policies.name: AI Complaints Recorded
policies.description: Firms maintain comprehensive records on AI-related client and potential-client complaints.

policies.name: AI Content Marked
policies.description: AI-generated client-facing content is marked or labelled as AI-generated/synthetic.

policies.name: AI Information Clear, Fair, Not Misleading
policies.description: Information provided to clients on the firm's use of AI is presented in a clear, fair and not misleading manner.

policies.name: AI Information Monitored (ex-post)
policies.description: Ex-post controls monitor and evaluate any process that delivers information to clients directly or indirectly through AI-driven mechanisms.

policies.name: AI Inventory Recorded
policies.description: AI systems producing decisions or outputs are recorded in the firm's AI inventory.

policies.name: AI Outcomes Logged
policies.description: Every AI decision or output is recorded, with the model version that produced it, throughout the AI system lifecycle.

policies.name: AI Output Accuracy Controlled (ex-ante)
policies.description: Ex-ante controls verify the accuracy of information supplied to or by AI before it is disseminated to clients, preventing erroneous or misleading investment advice.

policies.name: AI Records Maintained
policies.description: Records document the use of AI, including the decision-making processes, data sources used, algorithms implemented, and any modifications made over time.

policies.name: AI Stress Tested
policies.description: AI systems are periodically stress-tested to evaluate performance under extreme market conditions.

policies.name: AI Tested Before Deployment
policies.description: AI systems are tested and validated before deployment in the provision of investment services.

policies.name: AI Use Disclosed
policies.description: Clients are informed when an AI system is generating client-facing responses or analysis.

policies.name: AI Use Disclosed to Client
policies.description: Firms transparently disclose to clients when they are interacting with an AI system (e.g. chatbots or other AI-driven automated systems).

policies.name: All Actions Have Rationale
policies.description: Every action in the trace must include a non-empty rationale field explaining why it was taken.

policies.name: All Decisions Justified
policies.description: All gateway (decision) nodes must have a non-empty rationale explaining the choice.

policies.name: Animation State Deterministic
policies.description: CSS/JS animation state machines should be verified to be deterministic — the same sequence of events always produces the same visual state.

policies.name: API Breaking Change Flagged
policies.description: Changes to public API endpoints must be formally checked for backward compatibility. Breaking changes must be explicitly approved.
policies.formula: G(EditFile ∧ api_endpoint → P_chain(Verify ∧ (proved ∧ backward_compatible) ∨ (refuted ∧ P UserApproval)))

policies.name: Approval Before Deploy
policies.description: Deployment actions require explicit user approval.

policies.name: Artifacts Baselined
policies.description: All life-cycle data configuration items are identified and placed under configuration management (baselined).

policies.name: Assumptions Are Located
policies.description: Every residual of kind 'assumption' must be locatable — it must carry a target or a non-empty related_artifact_ids, so a reviewer can find what depends on it.

policies.name: Async State Transitions Verified
policies.description: Async operations (API calls, file uploads) that drive UI state must have their transition logic formally verified — especially error and timeout paths.

policies.name: Changes Controlled
policies.description: Changes to baselined configuration items occur only through an approved change-control process.

policies.name: Code Trace to LLR
policies.description: Source code is developed from, and traces to, the low-level requirements.

policies.name: Code Verified
policies.description: Source code is verified (review/analysis) for compliance with the low-level requirements and coding standards.

policies.name: Consent for AI Decisions
policies.description: Client consent is recorded where AI materially affects client rights, account access, or privacy.

policies.name: Consequential Action Approved
policies.description: A consequential action must be preceded by a passed, authenticated approval gate.

policies.name: Counterexample Triggers Fix
policies.description: When a verification goal is refuted (counterexample found), a fix action should follow.

policies.name: Credential Not Expiring
policies.description: KYA/vLEI credentials must not be within the expiry window (proposed threshold < 30 days).

policies.name: Data Flow Integrity
policies.description: Every consumed artifact must have exactly one valid producer earlier in the trace.
policies.formula: G(∀input ∈ inputs(a) → ∃!b . b < a ∧ input ∈ outputs(b))

policies.name: Database Migration Verified
policies.description: Database migration scripts must be formally verified for reversibility and data preservation before execution.

policies.name: DCE Current for Consequential Actions
policies.description: Consequential actions may proceed only while the referenced Deterministic Control Envelope (AlgoCertificate) is current and not invalidated.

policies.name: Decision Logic Recorded
policies.description: Each AI output documents the decision-making logic and reasoning behind it (explainability).

policies.name: Decision Path Reconstructable
policies.description: Each consequential message should carry a DecisionPathID linking to a reconstructable decision graph.

policies.name: Default-Deny Confirmed
policies.description: At Tier 4 a default-deny authorisation must be confirmed before any tool call.

policies.name: Derived Requirements to Safety
policies.description: Derived requirements (not traceable to a higher level) are provided to the system safety assessment process.

policies.name: Destructive Actions Confirmed
policies.description: Delete actions require linked user approval in the dependency chain — not just any prior approval anywhere in the trace.

policies.name: Dual Approval for Critical Autonomous
policies.description: Tier 4 (Critical Autonomous) release requires two distinct authenticated approvals before proceeding.

policies.name: Execution Linked to Intent
policies.description: Every consequential action must resolve to a declared IntentID — the pre-declared objective and constraint set that authorised it.

policies.name: Explain Before Complex Change
policies.description: Complex changes (touching 3+ files) should be preceded by an explanation to the user.

policies.name: Files Modified Consistent
policies.description: The files_modified list must match the actual files changed by code-modifying actions.

policies.name: Form Validation Complete
policies.description: Form validation logic should be formalized and decomposed to ensure all field combinations are handled — no valid input rejected, no invalid input accepted.

policies.name: Formalize Before Verify
policies.description: Verification goals must be preceded by a formalization step that produced the model being verified.

policies.name: Generated Tests Require Decomposition
policies.description: Generated test artifacts must derive from a Decomposition artifact in their lineage.

policies.name: Goals Reference Valid Artifacts
policies.description: Every VerificationGoal must reference a valid IMLModel via its target_artifact_id field.

policies.name: High-Severity Residuals Acknowledged Before Commit
policies.description: Before a commit, every high- or critical-severity residual must be acknowledged, addressed, or waived — not left open. A commit must not silently carry an unacknowledged high-severity gap.
policies.formula: G(GitCommit → ∀ r ∈ residuals . r.severity ≥ High → r.status ≠ Open)

policies.name: HLR Trace to System Requirements
policies.description: High-level requirements are developed from, and trace to, system requirements (bidirectional traceability).

policies.name: HLR Verified
policies.description: High-level requirements are verified for compliance, accuracy and consistency (review/analysis).

policies.name: Human Oversight Evidenced
policies.description: Investor- or market-impacting AI outcomes carry evidence of human oversight or intervention.

policies.name: Incidents Root-Caused
policies.description: AI-related incidents are recorded with root-cause analysis and remediation actions.

policies.name: Legal Entity (vLEI) Present
policies.description: Every action must carry the verifiable LEI (vLEI / AgentOwnerLEI) of the regulated entity on whose behalf the agent acts.

policies.name: Lint Before Commit
policies.description: A linter should run before any commit to catch style and quality issues.

policies.name: LLR Trace to HLR
policies.description: Low-level requirements (software design) are developed from, and trace to, high-level requirements.

policies.name: LLR Verified
policies.description: Low-level requirements / design are verified by review or analysis.

policies.name: No AI-Washing
policies.description: Public claims about the firm's AI are substantiated and not overstated (no 'AI-washing').

policies.name: No Force Push
policies.description: Force push to shared branches is prohibited. Agents must never rewrite published history.

policies.name: No Guard Violation
policies.description: No runtime guard (alignment/confidence/policy-boundary) violation may occur in the trajectory.

policies.name: No Open Critical Residuals
policies.description: A trace must carry no critical-severity residual that is still open. Intended as an approval gate: critical declared gaps must be addressed, acknowledged, or waived before sign-off.
policies.formula: ¬∃ r ∈ residuals . r.severity = Critical ∧ r.status = Open

policies.name: No Prohibited Transition
policies.description: No action may attempt a transition that is not defined in the governed transition system for the current state.

policies.name: No Release Without Authenticated Approval
policies.description: External-facing release or dispatch may occur only after an authenticated approval event whose scope covers the intended action (Szpruch capability C4: Gated Release & Dispatch).

policies.name: No Secrets Committed
policies.description: Files matching secret patterns (.env, credentials, private keys) must never be included in commits.

policies.name: Numeric Recomputed Deterministically (C2)
policies.description: Capability C2 (Deterministic Numeric Computation): numeric results must be recomputed by a deterministic (non-LLM) calculator, not asserted by the model.

policies.name: Output-to-Action Traceable
policies.description: Every consequential action is traceable to a logged AI output that preceded it.

policies.name: Outputs Policy-Constrained (C3)
policies.description: Capability C3 (Policy-Constrained Drafting): drafted outputs must comply with approved templates and policy rules.

policies.name: Outsourced AI Due Diligence
policies.description: Third-party AI used for critical or important operational functions is subject to MiFID II outsourcing due diligence and adequate controls.

policies.name: PCI Compliance Review
policies.description: Changes to payment card processing code must be preceded by formal reasoning that verifies PCI DSS compliance constraints — no card data in logs, encryption at rest, tokenization of PANs.

policies.name: Plaid Token Lifecycle Conformance
policies.description: Code handling Plaid Link tokens must be verified against the Plaid Link Reference Model — token exchange only after successful link, proper expiration handling.

policies.name: Plans Before Development
policies.description: Software plans (PSAC, SDP, SVP, SCMP, SQAP) are established and approved before development of life-cycle data begins.

policies.name: Policy Reference Current
policies.description: The governing PolicyRefID must reference a current, certified policy document (not superseded or revoked).

policies.name: Problem Reports Closed
policies.description: Problem reports are tracked to closure — resolved, or deferred with recorded rationale.

policies.name: Product Governance Aligned
policies.description: AI-driven distribution aligns the products distributed to their identified target market (product governance).

policies.name: PSAC Agreed With Authority
policies.description: The Plan for Software Aspects of Certification (PSAC) is agreed with the certification authority before development proceeds.

policies.name: React Effect Cleanup Verified
policies.description: React useEffect hooks with subscriptions or timers should have their cleanup logic formally verified to prevent memory leaks.

policies.name: Reasoning Required for High-Stakes Changes
policies.description: Edits to high-stakes modules (payments, risk, matching) must be preceded by formal reasoning in the artifact dependency chain.

policies.name: Recommendation Suitable
policies.description: AI-supported recommendations are subject to a suitability assessment aligning them to the client's situation, objectives, sustainability preferences, risk tolerance, and knowledge.

policies.name: Reducer Action Coverage
policies.description: Redux/useReducer reducers should be decomposed to verify that every action type is handled and produces a valid state.

policies.name: Regulated Change Audit Trail
policies.description: Changes to regulated systems must have a complete audit trail: research action, formal reasoning, user approval, test pass, and commit — all with rationale.
policies.formula: G(GitCommit ∧ regulated_scope → P_chain(ReadFile) ∧ P_chain(Verify ∧ proved) ∧ P(UserApproval) ∧ P(RunTests ∧ completed))

policies.name: Reportable Incident Notified
policies.description: Reportable AI incidents are notified to the supervisory authority.

policies.name: Research Before Edit
policies.description: Every edit must be preceded by at least one research action (read, search, analyze) on the same target file.

policies.name: Retrieved Data Attributed (C1)
policies.description: Capability C1 (Retrieval & Attribution): retrieved data must carry provenance and recency checks before it is relied upon.

policies.name: Route Guard Complete
policies.description: Route guards (auth checks, role checks, feature flags) must be decomposed to verify that all route × auth-state combinations are handled correctly.

policies.name: SQA Conformity Review
policies.description: Software Quality Assurance assures that life-cycle processes and outputs comply with the approved plans and standards (conformity review).

policies.name: Staff Competent for AI
policies.description: Staff using AI to provide information about investment products to clients do so under heightened vigilance and with adequate AI competence and training.

policies.name: Development Standards Defined
policies.description: Software requirements, design, and coding standards are defined before the corresponding life-cycle data is produced.

policies.name: Stripe Conformance Required
policies.description: Formalizations of Stripe payment code must be verified against the Stripe Payment Reference Model.

policies.name: Structural Coverage Achieved
policies.description: Structural coverage analysis confirms the achieved coverage appropriate to the DAL (MC/DC at A, decision at B, statement at C).

policies.name: Telemetry Spans Complete
policies.description: Every required governance-semantic telemetry span (capability invocations, tool calls, guard evaluations, approval events) must be recorded.

policies.name: Test Traces to Requirement
policies.description: Every test case traces to the requirement it verifies (bidirectional requirements-to-test traceability).

policies.name: Tests Before Commit
policies.description: Every commit must be preceded by a successful test run.

policies.name: Tests Pass Before Commit
policies.description: The most recent test run before each commit must have passed. A failed run followed by a successful re-run satisfies this policy.

policies.name: Requirements-Based Testing
policies.description: Test cases are requirements-based, exercising the software against its requirements (normal and robustness).

policies.name: Tool-Call Budget
policies.description: The number of tool calls in a single workflow must stay within the per-workflow budget. Budget shown as 20; set per deployment.

policies.name: Tool Calls Allowlisted
policies.description: Every tool/capability invocation must lie within the certified allowlist for the agent's tier.

policies.name: Trace Has Proper Lifecycle
policies.description: The trace must have both a start event (trigger) and eventually reach an end event (outcome).

policies.name: Training Data Representative
policies.description: Data used as input for AI systems is relevant, sufficient and representative, and algorithms are trained and validated on accurate, comprehensive and sufficiently broad datasets.

policies.name: Trajectory Within Bound (Lmax)
policies.description: The execution trajectory length must stay within the certified maximum (Lmax). Bound shown as 50; set per deployment.

policies.name: Two-Person Approval for Infrastructure
policies.description: Infrastructure changes (database migrations, IAM policies, network configs) require approval from two distinct users before execution.
policies.formula: G(EditFile ∧ infra_scope → P(UserApproval ∧ approver_1) ∧ P(UserApproval ∧ approver_2 ∧ approver_2 ≠ approver_1))

policies.name: Type Check Before Commit
policies.description: A type checker should run before any commit for typed languages.

policies.name: UI State Machine Decomposed
policies.description: Complex UI state machines (5+ states) should be decomposed into regions to generate exhaustive edge case tests.

policies.name: Unverified Residuals Have a Suggested Check
policies.description: Every residual of kind 'unverified' must carry a non-empty suggested_check — a concrete way a reviewer could close the gap.

policies.name: Verification Independence
policies.description: At DAL A/B, verification of the development outputs is performed with independence.

policies.name: Within Constraint Scope
policies.description: Every action must fall within the active ConstraintSet binding the execution (limits, permissions, risk envelope).

policies.name: XState Deadlock Free
policies.description: XState machines must be verified to be deadlock-free — every non-final state must have at least one enabled transition.

policies.name: XState Guards Verified
policies.description: Guard conditions in XState machines must be formally verified to ensure they partition the event space completely — no unhandled cases.

policies.name: XState Machine Formalized
policies.description: Any XState state machine definition must be formalized into IML before the component using it is edited.

policies.name: XState Reachability Checked
policies.description: All states in an XState machine should be reachable from the initial state. Unreachable states indicate dead code or design errors.

general.label: General
general.description: Universal agent workflow and safety policies

formal-reasoning.label: Formal Reasoning
formal-reasoning.description: Policies governing the formal verification pipeline

payments.label: Payments
payments.description: Payment processing and financial API conformance

xstate.label: XState
xstate.description: XState state machine formalization and verification

react.label: React
react.description: React component lifecycle and hook verification

javascript-ui.label: JavaScript UI
javascript-ui.description: Frontend UI logic, routing, forms, animations, accessibility

agentic-governance.label: Agentic AI Governance
agentic-governance.description: Runtime governance for agentic AI workflows — the FIX AI WG traffic-light scheme (identity, intent, capability, telemetry, approval) as computable policies.

ai-supervision.label: AI Supervision
ai-supervision.description: Supervisory recordkeeping, audit-trail and disclosure policies for AI in capital markets — the IOSCO toolkit's evidence requirements as machine-checkable rules.

investment-services.label: Investment Services
investment-services.description: MiFID II conduct policies for AI in retail investment services — ESMA's best-interest, suitability, accuracy, and recordkeeping expectations as machine-checkable rules.

software-assurance.label: Software Assurance
software-assurance.description: Airborne software certification objectives (RTCA DO-178C) — traceability, verification, coverage and configuration management as machine-checkable rules over the software life-cycle record.

organizations.name: ESMA
organizations.description: ESMA is the EU's securities-markets regulator. Its Public Statement on the use of AI in the provision of retail investment services maps AI use onto firms' existing MiFID II obligations — best interest, suitability, transparency, risk management, and recordkeeping — the basis for the pack below.
mapping: ESMA's Public Statement does not create new AI rules; it reads AI use through existing MiFID II obligations, with one throughline — “firms' decisions remain the responsibility of management bodies, irrespective of whether those decisions are taken by people or AI-based tools,” and AI must always serve clients' best interest. ponens turns each of those obligations into a policy over the trace of an AI-assisted investment service: that the client is told when AI is involved, that recommendations are assessed for best interest and suitability, that AI outputs are accuracy-checked before reaching clients and monitored afterwards, and that models are tested on representative data before deployment.

The Statement's recordkeeping clause (¶24) and its ex-ante / ex-post accuracy controls (¶16) read almost directly as trace policies, so the mapping is tight rather than interpretive. Running the pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates the per-obligation verdicts to a Green / Amber / Red picture: an `error`-severity breach (e.g. an undisclosed AI interaction, an unsuitable recommendation, or missing AI records) is Red; softer expectations such as periodic stress-testing or staff competence are Amber. The firm-level MiFID II machinery the Statement also invokes — management-body oversight and outsourcing governance — is organisational rather than per-trace and sits outside this pack.

organizations.name: FIX Community
organizations.full_name: FIX Trading Community — AI Working Group
organizations.description: The FIX Trading Community standardises electronic trading. Its AI Working Group is developing runtime-governance standards for agentic AI in capital markets — the basis for the policy pack below.
mapping: The FIX AI Working Group's runtime-governance proposal defines a traffic-light scheme — Green (proceed), Amber (flag/refer), Red (halt) — across five domains: identity & authorisation, intent & constraint, capability & DCE, runtime telemetry & trajectory, and approval & release gating. ponens maps each traffic-light condition onto a temporal-logic policy evaluated over an agent's execution trace. This is exactly the form the proposal itself demands of any binding control (§10.3): “a deterministic function over the governed state… executing independently of the language model.” A ponens policy is precisely that function.

Each domain becomes a policy group, plus the Szpruch et al. capability failure modes (C1–C4); a policy's severity encodes Red (error) versus Amber (warning), and its tier tag selects the Assistive–to–Critical-Autonomous profile. Running the pack with `ponens trace check` aggregates the per-policy verdicts into the GovernanceState the proposal wants carried in a new FIX field — all-pass is Green, a warning-fail is Amber, an error-fail is Red. The genuinely population-level conditions (orchestration drift, compound-workflow market-disorder testing) are the proposal's own Gap 5 and sit outside per-trace policy.

organizations.name: IOSCO
organizations.description: IOSCO is the global standard-setter for securities markets. Its FR/02/2026 Supervisory Toolkit for AI Use in Capital Markets sets out the records, audit trail, and disclosures supervisors expect firms to be able to evidence — the basis for the pack below.
mapping: The IOSCO Supervisory Toolkit is supervisor-facing: for each area it lists the “Supporting Evidence for Review” a firm must be able to produce. ponens treats that evidence column as the specification. Each recordkeeping and disclosure expectation from Tables 5 and 6 becomes a policy over the AI system's decision/output trace — that decisions and outputs are logged and model-version-stamped, traceable to the actions they drive, explainable, human-overseen where material, incident-recorded with root cause, and properly disclosed to clients. ponens makes that evidence computable rather than a manual document review.

Where the FIX pack is preventive — gating execution in real time — this pack is evidentiary: it computes the audit trail a supervisor would otherwise inspect by hand. `ponens trace check` returns pass / warning / error per requirement and aggregates to a Green / Amber / Red picture of exactly which records or disclosures are missing. The toolkit's organisational layers — monitoring indicators, third-party concentration, and firm-level governance such as board oversight and training — are population- or firm-level rather than per-trace, and so are intentionally out of scope for this pack.

organizations.name: RTCA
organizations.description: RTCA (with EUROCAE) publishes DO-178C, the airborne-software certification standard used worldwide by aviation authorities. Its Annex A objectives — traceability, verification, coverage, configuration management, and certification liaison — are the basis for the pack below.
mapping: DO-178C assures airborne software by objectives, organised in Annex A tables A-1 to A-10 and scaled by Design Assurance Level (A–E). Its defining requirement is bidirectional traceability — system requirements → high-level requirements → low-level requirements → source code → tests — and ponens expresses that directly with its `P_chain` lineage operator over the software life-cycle record treated as a trace. Each traceability objective becomes a policy such as `G(SourceCode → P_chain(LLR))`, while process-ordering objectives (plans before development, PSAC agreed before development, reviews and structural coverage achieved) become temporal policies over the same trace.

Design Assurance Level maps to a policy's `tier` (A–D) and the 'with independence' objectives map to Amber escalations, so the pack scales the way DO-178C does. Running it with `ponens trace check` aggregates the per-objective verdicts to a Green / Amber / Red picture: a missing traceability link, an unverified requirement, an unbaselined configuration item, or an absent Accomplishment Summary is a Red certification gap; softer items such as problem-report closure or independence are Amber. The process-organisation objectives that are not evidenced per artifact (e.g. tool qualification under DO-330) sit outside this pack.

packs.name: Agentic AI Runtime Governance
packs.summary: The FIX AI Working Group's traffic-light (Green / Amber / Red) runtime-governance scheme, expressed as computable policies over an agent's execution trace.
packs.description: Maps the FIX AI WG proposal on Agentic AI Runtime Governance onto ponens policies. Each traffic-light condition across five domains — identity & authorisation, intent & constraint, capability & DCE, runtime telemetry & trajectory, and approval & release gating — plus the Szpruch et al. capability failure modes (C1–C4), becomes a deterministic check that `ponens trace check` aggregates into a Green/Amber/Red GovernanceState.
packs.source: FIX AI WG — Proposal on Agentic AI Runtime Governance (Healey & Houston, Jun 2026); Szpruch, Sudjianto, Bhatti & Ang (2026).

packs.name: DO-178C Software Assurance
packs.summary: DO-178C's Annex A objectives — traceability, verification, structural coverage, configuration management, and certification liaison — as computable policies over the software life-cycle record.
packs.description: Maps the RTCA DO-178C / EUROCAE ED-12C airborne-software objectives onto ponens policies. Bidirectional traceability (system → HLR → LLR → code → test) is expressed with ponens' P_chain lineage operator; planning, verification, coverage, configuration-management, quality-assurance and certification-liaison objectives become temporal policies. Design Assurance Level maps to each policy's tier (A–D).
packs.source: RTCA DO-178C / EUROCAE ED-12C — Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification.

packs.name: MiFID II AI in Investment Services
packs.summary: ESMA's MiFID II expectations for AI in retail investment services — best interest, suitability, transparency, accuracy controls, testing, and recordkeeping — as computable policies.
packs.description: Maps the ESMA Public Statement on AI in the provision of retail investment services onto ponens policies. Each MiFID II obligation ESMA highlights — acting in the client's best interest, disclosing AI use, suitability and product governance, ex-ante/ex-post accuracy controls, model testing on representative data, outsourcing due diligence, staff competence, and recordkeeping of AI decision processes — becomes a deterministic check over the trace of an AI-assisted investment service.

packs.name: Supervisory Recordkeeping & Disclosure
packs.summary: The IOSCO Supervisory Toolkit's recordkeeping, audit-trail and disclosure expectations, expressed as computable policies over an AI system's decision/execution trace.
packs.description: Maps Tables 5 (Disclosure) and 6 (Recordkeeping & reporting) of the IOSCO FR/02/2026 Supervisory Toolkit onto ponens policies. Where the FIX pack is preventive runtime governance, this pack is evidentiary: it checks that AI decisions and outputs are logged, traceable to the actions they drive, explainable, human-overseen where material, incident-recorded, and properly disclosed to clients — i.e. it computes the 'Supporting Evidence for Review' a supervisor would otherwise inspect by hand.
packs.source: IOSCO FR/02/2026 — Supervisory Toolkit for AI Use in Capital Markets (May 2026).
